item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The fact that the Foundation is using Python to push their database scares me.
Oh, and everything else about this article holy shit that last bit fucked with me, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There is something very off putting about this article that really does it for me. +1

The ending of this scip is pretty disappointing, but very successful at being creepy. I don't like scips which suddenly info dump at the end so on the one hand I'm glad you don't do that, but on the other it's a shame that more is steadily revealed only to end on the fairly information deprived note of the video. However, I really like your explanation on the first discusson page and the effect of the 4182 picture zooming in closer and closer is incredibly effective.

item.title: like.py
item.content:encoded:
I approve of the Python traceback, and adore anything to do with that language. The rest of the skip being wonderful (particularly your explanation in the comments clarifying your thinking, and the /offset/# rabbit hole) is just the icing on the cake.

edit: though, I do have to agree with Lambda Fairy about the HTTP status code thing. If you can write a fictitious Python trace back and an EXCELLENT computer-centric 001 proposal, you should know better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Initially, it refers to the anomaly's effect (the rate at which references to "Site-5" appear). However, this becomes more uncertain over each iteration, since what SCP-4182 refers to starts to change. It starts to sound like it's referring to the island itself — as if the island may be "occurring" at an increased rate. Cue image of the island itself just spreading like a physical memetic effect.

This ambiguity is semi-intentional. That is to say: It wasn't intentional at first, but when I later noticed the ambiguity, I decided to keep it in because I liked how it might baffle the reader and deepen the sense of confusion and intrigue.

YMMV, of course. The line between "good ambiguity" and "bad ambiguity" is very fuzzy — particularly when it comes to horror.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The thing I still don't get is what you mean by "SCP-4182's rate of occurrence has been increasing." In each iteration, SCP-4182 seems to refer to just the island. So, what exactly is occurring at an increasing rate? The re-discovery of the island and/or its documents? Some aspect of the phenomenon, left unexplained, that led to the island being closed off and buried?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Largest frighten in a while. The images of Site-5 getting closer and closer with each iteration had a much bigger effect on me than I expected. Enthusiastic +1

item.title: Small nitpick
item.content:encoded: An Internal Server Error would have a code of 500, not 404.

item.title: Classification
item.content:encoded: Now I wonder about classification. When we had an artificial island posing as Site-5 with subterranean complex with something extremely dangerous inside, it was Keter, and that's understandable. When we had only an artificial island posing as Site-5 with something unknown inside (paneling the stairways, sealing the buildings), it was Euclid. When we only had anomaly referencing to Site-5, but the artificial island had disappeared, it suddenly became Keter again. Why?

item.title: Re: there IS a site-05, though. and a site-005. you need to be more specific.
item.content:encoded: How can they be "down there" if buildings are sealed and stairways are plugged with concrete? One person surely wouldn't be able to break through it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've yet to watch the video at the end, but would you be interested in making the email message flash like in SCP-4514 and SCP-4415? Not sure if this has been suggested.

+ show block
– hide block
 [[module CSS]] .blinkbar {color: red; font-size:125%; animation: blink 1.5s infinite; letter-spacing: -0.13em;}@keyframes blink{to{opacity: .0;}}@keyframes flicker {0% {opacity: 0.9890; }5% {opacity: 0.5842; }10% {opacity: 0.9865; }20% {opacity: 0.0412; }20% {opacity: 0.6255; }25% {opacity: 0.3157; }30% {opacity: 0.7328; }35% {opacity: 0.4654; }40% {opacity: 0.9128; }45% {opacity: 0.2449; }50% {opacity: 0.5485; }55% {opacity: 0.6438; }60% {opacity: 0.1180; }65% {opacity: 0.9085; }70% {opacity: 0.0266; }75% {opacity: 0.6795; }80% {opacity: 0.7812; }85% {opacity: 0.0117; }90% {opacity: 0.2239; }95% {opacity: 0.8897; }100% {opacity: 0.2260; }}@keyframes overlay-anim {0% {visibility: hidden; }10% {visibility: hidden; }11% {visibility: visible; }50% {visibility: hidden; }100% {visibility: hidden; }} #page-content .collapsible-block-folded a.collapsible-block-link { animation: blink 0.8s ease-in-out infinite alternate; } @keyframes blink { 0% { color: transparent; } 50%, 100% { color: #b01; } } [[/module]]

Also this is really amazing, thanks for posting!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't believe I forgot to upvote when this came out.

item.title: Re: there IS a site-05, though. and a site-005. you need to be more specific.
item.content:encoded: What are they doing there? Are they a traitor? Or a part of the anomaly? Or do they just want to raise awareness?

item.title: Re: there IS a site-05, though. and a site-005. you need to be more specific.
item.content:encoded: Part of my head canon is that J Davis is definitely down there in that hole. That's what "see you soon ~:)" is meant to imply.

item.title: Re: there IS a site-05, though. and a site-005. you need to be more specific.
item.content:encoded:
Interesting though. I previously thought that SCP-4182 contained some extremely dangerous anomaly, which lured people there, this is why the staff needed to be reminded that Site-5 does not exist, which is why stairways needed to be plugged with concrete and buildings sealed.

But if it's really something that needs to be hidden, then it's the failure to hide that's really interesting. As I understood, the anomaly forces the Foundation to forget about SCP-4182, which is why they attempt to enter sealed buildings and excavate stairways. And when they finally declared SCP-4182 to be a software bug to lessen the interest to an island, J. Davis posted a video from it. Meanwhile, who is J. Davis? Are they a part of the anomaly? Did the anomaly give them knowledge? Did the anomaly force them to post video?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Something here really reminds me of the Yucca Mountain problem, that is, how do we make humans, such a intrisically curious lot, just stop investigating something for their own good. I kinda wish that psychological/behavioural aspect was delved into a bit more here, but still a wonderful old school creepy piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just kept wondering where this SCP-1864 rewrite was going. The shared elements did not help the deja vu.

(I'm not saying this is a knock-off, just, you know, the wiki is old. I'll probably have more of an opinion tomorrow, I stopped the video halfway through because it was creepy and I intend to sleep tonight.)

Ok, I think it's probably due to the unintended and unfortunate comparison to Pan Hun, but this really didn't do much for me in the end. It's definitely atmospheric and did creep me out, but the tale you tell is all meta. The narrative is the cover-up and rediscovery, which was clever and had a lot of finicky little turns. But at the end of the day, the actual narrative is entirely withheld, and so it's just about a pile of melted bodies. I can't care about that.

Edit: No vote. You've got that prodding-at-a-loose-tooth tension dead to rights, I just wish it was in service of something more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It’s been a while since a scip actually gave me a fright. That video at the end really sells it. +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To me, it looked like human corpses. By Foundationverse standards, that's not particularly scary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry for the late reply, life got in the way and I wanted to mull the skip over a bit to be able to pinpoint exactly how I feel about it.

+ show block
– hide block

After trying to find the specific points, I came to realize my main issue linked to my growing wariness towards the Nothing is Scarier trope, especially when it comes to SCPs. The thing is that as a reader I need something to focus on, whether it be an important plot point, a character, or good atmosphere through explorations or discoveries, in order for the trope to work. Obfuscating the monster doesn't work if there is nothing for me to be wary of, it just makes me read through the story without noticing the danger the same way I could walk past a serial killer and not be scared because I didn't know. Even Lovecraft's most Nothing is Scarier stories leave me with something to grasp to. The Music of Erich Zann doesn't tell me what the monster is, but it lets me know that the only thing between it and the world is an old man with a viol. The Other Gods doesn't specify the exact nature of these new gods, but it does tell me that gods have their own gods, which is a concept uncanny enough to leave me wondering. There is something that is tangential enough that I can think it over without falling into the paradox of choice that happens when you give me an empty room and ask me to image the scariest thing I can.

Now the article obviously isn't like that, I was using extremes to try to get my view across, but I still feel the article isn't giving me anything concrete to focus on. There are many small details, yes, but they don't feel connected enough to point me anywhere. All I know are details surrounding the anomaly, not anything about it. The anomaly could be anything from a reality bender to a cognitohazard, and it could be world ending or simply know Foundation secrets. Telling me that the SCP is dangerous or that the Foundation is scared of it doesn't do anything for me because there are already so many other SCPs that are either dangerous or scary that my brain will go "yeah, something like that" and call it a day. Why should I bother trying to imagine the worst thing possible, when there's hundreds of articles that take the time to do it?

I read your 3219 and I can say that, aside from being pretty damn atmospheric and spooky, it did the main idea very well. Like this one, it doesn't tell me what the actual SCP is, but it does tell me a lot of what it does. I know people go missing in an area, it makes animals and people unconsciously (or consciously) not want to be near it, there is a connection with a house and a family, and there is something about the soil or the ground itself that is wrong. This not only points me at a direction to look at, the elements feel connected and give a sense of danger. I know I don't want to be near this place, but at the same time this unknown danger makes me want to know more. The article is scary enough for me that I instinctively want to know more not because I think it'll make it scarier, but because I feel knowing what's going on is the only way I might stop being unnerved by whatever it is, and that is why the piece shines for me.

4182's details are more curiosities than atmospheric. The different discovery date, the change in exclusion zone size, the change from sterilizing documents to people, and the Foundation being hush hush about it. These elements don't really tell me anything about the anomaly, aside from the fact that the Foundation wants it kept secret which, as I said before, is too vague for me to actually care when other SCPs go through with it. Even taken together I don't particularly feel any sense of danger or any building dread from them, since they seem so inconsequential. The picture of corpses and the video themselves are elements that could do this, but they feel too disconnected from the rest of the article in terms of atmosphere that I'm still drawing a blank as to what this is supposed to be.

Sorry if this reads rambly or like I'm drilling on you. The latter is unintentional and the former is that I want to try to get my feelings for the article as clear as possible. I like the idea (and I really, really like your explanation), but the article feels too much like an "empty room" for me to upvote. But I won't downvote because I love it when authors try new things and experiment, and because the article did run around in my mind for a few days, even if it wasn't for the intended reasons, and I think that's always a positive.

item.title: Re: there IS a site-05, though. and a site-005. you need to be more specific.
item.content:encoded: I absolutely loved this. It genuinely creeped me out and gave me severe goosebumps. The way the documents keep going back and back and then at the end it's an explained SCP and you think, "oh, okay. That simple." You look around for something else and you see the 1 new message and you click on it and it gave me ultimate chills when they guy said "they're lying." Fantastic piece of writing, although in the video it would have been nice to have a little scare I was waiting for something to burst out of that door at the end.

item.title: Re: there IS a site-05, though. and a site-005. you need to be more specific.
item.content:encoded:
I was initially off-put by certain aspects of the article and prepared to downvote, because this particular take on it didn't come to mind. I did see it as the Foundation trying to hide it, and previous iterations obviously being less and less redacted, but I thought that was a matter of them not wanting others to discover it (which was a bit obvious and lame if so). But I figured I should check the comments first to see if there was something I just wasn't seeing about it.

I didn't think of the notion that the Foundation was trying to bury it deeper because THEY didn't want to know about it, that they were hiding it from THEMSELVES. That thought legitimately takes this from being mediocre to being spectacular.

Enthusiastic upvote, though I wish there was some way to make that conclusion a bit easier to arrive at. The ghosts of your own past coming back to haunt you directly is a much more compelling story than just trying to hide your secrets from others. Something that refuses to allow itself to be completely forgotten. I love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can appreciate how that hurt the piece for you. The reason I axed them was because of a conscious decision I made to remove any actual 'voice' (as in, we never "hear" anyone talking or interacting with the site), but I understand how that decision could disengage some readers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agreed. In addition, from the first draft of this, the axing of the supplementary logs took out some depth and really hurt the piece for me.

Novote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thinking of the atrocious conditions the workers of Batteship Island had to put up with in the first place, this is an extremely fitting piece.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"see you there ~:)"

wow ok +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Atmospheric but a null vote, I feel you've perhaps left too much behind the curtain, the idea of the Foundation trying to bury something truly horrific from its past is a good one, but the limitation here is that the 'present' Foundation is unapologetically involved in some very dark things, what makes this so much worse is left to to the reader to infer, and what can be inferred doesn't really justify the degree of concealment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1) I loved seeing the island keep getting closer each time; a somewhat-but-not-really-but-still subtle, nice touch, as it got me wondering (while reading) what the last one would end up being.

2) I honestly would never have gotten the "main" point you call out in the author's notes (outside of The Foundation is behind all the secrecy, and they don't know why/at least not everyone knows why), there just, I feel, was not enough information provided to get that far. But, I can only obviously speak for myself.

3) I actually appreciated that the edited type wasn't blue like has been done so often before, it's become pretty cliche. If this was actual documentation (however this would actually work) that obvious calling out would not exist.

4) Well done overall, props for taking the effort to video it up, and while I may, after my initial read, had wished there was more info provided, I'm fine with having only bits to go on, and letting my imagination just deal with it.

item.title: Re: there IS a site-05, though. and a site-005. you need to be more specific.
item.content:encoded: Regrettably, not at the moment — I actually accidentally deleted it. I still have all the clips, though. I'll probably end up eventually posting the assets on reddit somewhere.

item.title: Re: there IS a site-05, though. and a site-005. you need to be more specific.
item.content:encoded: Does the original video exist anywhere? I enjoyed the first version more, although I understand why it was changed - the animal cry in particular greatly helped the atmosphere and the slow build of tension, in my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the idea behind this skip (the whole "you can't bury your past" thing), but I felt like not enough was done in the actual article to convey it. While minimalism and restraint definitely have their importance, I think I would've preferred just seeing more here; maybe something like e-mail exchanges between higher-ups and a staff member who's trying to dig a little too deep. As it is, the article didn't pique my interest all that much, and I felt that the video didn't really work too well as a hook due to the lack of solid build-up. No-vote for now — although I might change my mind later, who knows.

item.title: Re: there IS a site-05, though. and a site-005. you need to be more specific.
item.content:encoded:
Updated/changed the video to address the siren effect (also added a brief clip of the exterior island, and shortened the video considerably — since its length, I think, was ultimately distracting).

(I also found a better format to use — the video's just 4 megs instead of 7, which should make it easier for people with crappy ISPs)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Jesus Christ that island is coming straight for me! MARY! MARY GET MY GUN! NO THE ISLAND GUN!

Honestly, beautifully executed. Very classic concept, but you really took it the distance.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I interpreted "sterilize" to be "cleansed of infection", i.e. they take steps to remove the effect from them. Not quite a correct usage of the term, but that was what I thought they meant.

item.title: Re: there IS a site-05, though. and a site-005. you need to be more specific.
item.content:encoded:
So, I said that at some point I'd make a post describing what this skip is about and what's going on, here. Insert vague "author head-canon is irrelevant, your interpretation is just as valid as mine" prologue here:

EXPLANATION:
– hide block

This skip is about genocide denial.

This idea started way back when I was doing searches on the site and noticed there wasn't a "Site-5" anywhere. This immediately prompted me to try and write a skip that explained why — the result was an article I didn't feel satisfied with (for various reasons). I sat on the premise for a while.

Eventually, I had a new idea. I wanted to invoke the Department of Abnormalities without ever referencing them by name. The DoA (for those unfamiliar) is like the Foundation's dark, absent doppelganger — a specter of a past that never happened. Something another user (Scantron, aka Communism will win) once said about them always stuck with me: the DoA can be a metaphor for how we bury our horrible past so deeply that upon 'rediscovering' it, it looks like it belongs to someone else. This is what I realized I wanted to evoke.

With each revision, the Foundation rediscovers the site — and immediately ceases to investigate it. With each incident, they bury it a little deeper (plugging the stairs with concrete, paneling the doors and windows, deleting the files) — but it keeps coming back. People keep remembering.

I won't tell you what the blacked out text is, but I'll mention some words you might find if you peeked: "congealed", "mass", "irradiated". There's a reason the containment procedures suddenly start talking about sterilizing "personnel" instead of documents (to evoke mass sterilization campaigns). There's a reason this takes place in Japan (on Hashima Island, specifically). There's even a reason for the air sirens (connecting it to WW2).

Something so unspeakably horrible happened here that the Foundation has disassociated themselves from it in every way imaginable. This past is so toxic, so destructive, so corrosive that every time they rediscover it, it literally rots their brains until they bury it even deeper… only for it to seep through the cracks, and for them to rediscover it again.

SCP-4182 is about the idea of being haunted by a horrible curse, a ghost that you keep insisting isn't real — only to discover that the ghost is your very own.

*spooky noises*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I appreciate the feedback, and you giving it a chance; even if it doesn't work for you, hearing how and why is always helpful.

Obviously, this is me experimenting with atmosphere and restraint (I really like to find the line where too much is too much and too little is too little). I'd be shocked if it worked for everyone — but hearing from those for whom it didn't is still incredibly useful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Stellar work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like what you're going for, but it sadly didn't work for me. Maybe it's because I'm not in the right mood, or that the repeating format distracts from the actual information, but I wasn't particularly immersed in the story. I don't feel like I'm given enough to get invested into the story to actually want to find out what's going on, and instead I just have that vague sense of curiosity I have when I click any SCP, if that makes sense. Like I understand I'm supposed to try to wrap my head around what little information there is and try to solve the mystery myself, but there wasn't any point for me in which the article stopped building up and started to get going. The penultimate iteration was starting to get my attention but then it suddenly ended, and while I like the video as a spooky video by itself, I was left with the vague idea of "spooky island" and not enough to drive me forward to make me want to actively work out what's going on.

I'm going to wait to vote until I read this again while it's dark outside to see if the atmosphere sinks in better for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh no. I am spook. So spook. +1

My favorite part is definitely the pictures of the island getting closer and closer. Pretty unsettling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For a while I thought this was going to link to A Good Boy (001 Proposal) because of the whole "Site-5 does not exist" thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, come on.

Compared to some Junji Ito works, that comic
barely made me raise an eyebrow.
barely made me rotate an eyebrow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Are they...?
- Are they...?

…going down there to mass-breed until they die of thirst? Is that why the floor is an amorphous blob of nearly skeletal corpses? That's the only reason I can think of to sterilize personnel affected by it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Did none of you guys see the amorphous blob of mummified human remains at the final frame of the video?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

One of the things I wanted to achieve with the SCP was the notion of leading the reader deeper and deeper into perceiving Site-5 as a "real" place; this is why each iteration has the image of the island getting closer and closer (with the video being the climax — we're now on the island, physically exploring it). That being said, I remember thinking even as I was editing the video, 2 minutes is a bit much. The important aspect is the descent down the stairs — everything else is atmospheric fluff.

When I edit this thing later tonight/tomorrow (to deal with the sound issue), I'll probably try to see if I can cut the first half. I kept it because the scream you're hearing is actually something that genuinely happened (animals living in the building). I was so charmed by capturing something so organically spooky on camera that it felt weird cutting it.

The emoticon was to create tonal dissonance re: jdavis. I wanted the reader to be left confused about who he was, what he was doing, what his motivations are — or why he's been leading them along like this. To me, there's something very, very sinister about ending on such an innocuous smiley face.

Re: the repetitive nature, I get that; it's one of the reasons each iteration is so short (the longest clocks in somewhere under 500-600 words). I didn't want to use blue text, though, because I feel blue text calls attention to the fiction of the document itself — and I wanted to get the reader as deeply into a realistic space as possible.

ETA: One thing I could change is reduce the size of the errors, which are actually just fluff and exist only to move the story along. I'd want to find something similarly 'real' looking, aesthetically, to replace them with though.

I appreciate the feedback!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are parts of this that I like, but there's a lot of things here that just really bother me.

The first thing that I noticed when reading this is that there's a lot of repetition between the fragments. There's not much that actually changes between the fragments just in terms of words. Usually about half of the words seem to remain the same (I haven't done the math, but I think half isn't too far off). My issue with this is that like, as a reader I started scanning to find what's different. It felt less like I was trying to read through the whole document and more like I was trying to just find what's changed. I think this could be easily fixed by like, just highlighting the changed bits in blue, although there's a large part of me that wishes there was just a more substantial change between each fragment. I say this because right now, while I do believe you have an intended reason why you divide each of the fragments up like this, it's really hard for me to figure out what is so important about the difference between the boarded up version, and the sealed version.

The other thing that bothers me is the video. It's a really nice video. Like, I like it a lot. But I feel like it doesn't actually like… give me any new information in and of itself. The bit at the very end is just a reference to something you effectively tell the reader in the second to last fragment, and like… unless you're much more clever than I'm giving you credit for, the leading 2 minutes of that clip appear to be just atmosphere effectively? Like I couldn't discern any details that really changed my understanding or intuition behind the story you're trying to tell. It does a really good job at building ambiance, but I guess I'm really just echoing ARD's point that you don't really do anything with the tension it builds.

Finally… why did you end it with that emoticon? I'm really curious about this because any form of emoticon feels really tonally dissonant from the mood you've built throughout the rest of the piece. Like, it feels playful and mischievous, but not in a way that contrasts well with the video that precedes it (at least in my opinion). I'm very curious why you put it there, because I have the feeling there's a specific reason and I'm just not getting it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reminds me of 087 in the best ways. It might have been the black and white imagery and all the subtle differences, but I was also reminded somewhat of Kalinin's Seven Strangers at a Feast. Easy +1.

Also fuck you for linking that awesome and intensely scary comic about pretty much everything I've ever been afraid of

EDIT: Dogs make proper formatting difficult.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm gonna take an even harder stance and say that I'm glad the other shoe never dropped. The tension is never resolved, and I'll be forever left wondering how Hippo got into Silent Hill what is so utterly wrong with this place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll see what I can do about this — I want the audio to be fucked up at the end, but I don't want to hurt anyone's ears (or even startle them, really). It might be a while before I can get to it, but I can probably reduce the volume during the final bits without losing the dramatic effect I want.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: what mom leave me alone I'll clean my room tomorrow

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This, along with DrSevere's summary, is basically the temporal element going on, yeah; some other important bits to remember here:

  • Every revision includes a different discovery date.
  • Every revision includes observations that are later (earlier) done as containment procedures. We note these weird panels that we wonder who put there …only for us to learn that we did, during the previous revision.
  • The Foundation keeps rediscovering it.
  • The Foundation keeps deciding to stop examining it, and never tells us why.
  • There's a subtle but extremely important change in the containment procedure of the penultimate entry (we go from sterilizing documents to…)
  • I'll probably make a post about what I actually intended this to be about later today, but I want to emphasize that no one has to "get" it, and the first priority is (as always) to be entertaining.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: hippo jesus christ that video

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not gonna throw shade at someone because their incredibly atmospheric footage of an actual exploration of a creepy abandoned building didn't have a good enough jump scare at the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have no idea what's going on, which obviously means I'm going to be checking the discussion page every once in a while until other people figure out what's going on.
It's creepy and interesting enough that I'm fine with not having a smidge of idea what's going on for now. +1.

One thing though - I think that the audio at the end spikes up to be way too loud way too quickly, which is somewhat of a detriment since it actually makes watching it a bit of a hassle. (I know I can just lower the volume, which I did, but I still think it's a bit poor.) Maybe use a bit of clipping to create the feeling that the audio is getting louder without actually making it louder.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The earliest revision suggests the Site-5 stuff was just a glitch,

While most revisions say "This document has encountered an unexpected condition. Click here to restore the previous revision, [..]", the second-to-last revision says "This document has received a recent revision. Click here to view the most recent version, [..]", meaning that the last revision is the earliest one.
This, along with the email that says "They're lying," is implying (I think) that your crawling of old revisions has been spotted, and you've been linked to a new revision in order to attempt and fool you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very cool, great assets. That said, I'm not quite sure I get it. Here's what I think is going on, please correct me if I've missed something:

We see the revisions in reverse chronological order - most recent first, then going back one at a time. The earliest revision suggests the Site-5 stuff was just a glitch, but then it turns out it actually was pointing to a real island. And then we see that with each subsequent revision, the mandated exclusion zone is getting bigger, and more and more steps are being taken to prevent personnel from entering. And I guess knowledge of it (maybe laying eyes on it?) makes you think it's real and want to go? And then you get there and your brain melts. So now the most recent ones scrub all information about it and just say there is no real place, and that the information about it just keeps cropping up (possibly actually being edited to be included by someone in the Foundation under its effect?).

Either way, the art assets and weirdness are enough for an easy +1, I'm just trying to figure out if I'm following correctly or not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whoooooo boy this was a goodie

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm heading to bed, but I just wanted to mention that this actually was inspired by a Junji Ito piece — Thing That Drifted Ashore.

(but there's also a very specific thing going on here. Kind of like SCP-3219, I wanted to see if I could successfully tell a story by leaving out the most relevant chunks.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I’m upvoting on the strength of how much this feels like a Junji Ito piece, but the video is a cop-out — there’s a lot of tension built and then nothing is done with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was fucking solid +1, genuine fear was acquired.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the mad lad
